summary: Asparouhova critiques voting as a governance mechanism, arguing it was designed for 18th-century conditions that no longer apply. She identifies three core problems: voting can never be truly representative (especially in decentralized communities where total population is unknown), it promotes competition over cooperation, and it creates a veneer of objectivity that absolves people of genuine compromise. She proposes cooperative governance as an alternative, drawing inspiration from cooperative board games and modern open source communities that use lazy consensus and consensus-seeking processes. The key insight is that cooperative mechanics flip incentives—making objections expensive rather than cheap—which could better suit our attention-scarce, reputation-driven era.
key_points: Voting was invented for small, homogenous populations (~1 million eligible voters in 1800 America) and doesn't scale to global, decentralized communities where total population is unknown
Voting is a zero-sum competitive game that promotes faction formation and conflict rather than cooperation, with no checks on well-coordinated factions spanning branches of government
Modern open source communities like Node.js and Rust have pioneered 'lazy consensus' and 'consensus seeking' as alternatives where voting is considered the failure outcome
Cooperative games work because they make vetoing expensive (requiring reputation to raise objections) while voting makes disagreement cheap, leading to performative outrage
Reputation is becoming more powerful than money as a form of influence, and cooperative governance leverages this by letting attention and expertise determine influence
money_quotes: If we're coming up with wild ideas for social coordination, voting seems like an awfully familiar solution.

Over-engineered systems create a 'veneer of objectivity' that lets us put blind faith into something greater than ourselves—and in doing so, absolve ourselves of the very real, hard work of disagreeing and compromising with our fellow humans.
If humans are good at one thing, it is circumvention.
It seems paradoxical to call decentralization 'revolutionary' while also preaching government by automation.
Voting is a last resort. A healthy community should rarely need to vote.
Under voting, it's cheap to veto, which has led to an anti-pattern of outrage today. Not only is it cheap to disagree, but you're often rewarded for it.
Votes can be manipulated by propaganda and special interests. Reputation takes time to build, but it belongs to you.
Voting wasn't designed for scale, and it's not going to carry us through the next era of governance.
key_insight: Cooperative governance mechanisms that make objections expensive and consensus the default state are better suited to our modern era of unknown populations, scarce attention, and reputation-based influence than the competitive, zero-sum voting systems designed for 18th-century conditions.
